meta_data.description: 4,093 likes, 70 comments - middleeasteye on March 23, 2026: "In a series of posts on X, Iran’s parliament speaker, Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, has repeated Tehran’s denial that talks took place between Iran and the US. On Monday Morning, US President Donald Trump backed down from threats to bomb Iranian power plants following what he described as “very good and productive conversations” with Iran. The Iranian foreign ministry denied these conversations had taken place and the Islamic Republic’s embassy in Kabul said Trump had backed down from striking power plants after Iran warned the US it would bomb energy sites across the Middle East. Later on Monday, Trump told reporters on the tarmac at West Palm Beach airport in Florida that Iran “very much wants to make a deal” and that “we would like to make a deal too”. He said that his two main negotiators, US Special Envoy to the Middle East Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner, his son-in-law, had led “very strong talks” with Iranian counterparts. Trump said that Witkoff was talking to “a top person” in Iran. “Don’t forget we’ve wiped out the leadership phase one, phase two and largely phase three,” the US president said. Asked if this “top person” was the new supreme leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, Trump said that it was not and claimed that Khamenei might be dead. He said he did not want to say who the US was negotiating with because he didn’t want that person to be killed. The talks, Trump said, took place on Sunday and would continue, “probably by phone” because, he said, it was hard for Iranian leaders to leave the country. He said that at some point soon US and Iranian leaders would meet in person. US allies in the Middle East were, Trump said, “very much in mind”.".

Reports indicate that US special envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner are negotiating with the speaker of the Iranian parliament, Ghalibaf. Several countries are attempting to convene a meeting that may take place in Islamabad, Pakistan. US Vice President JD Vance could also participate in this meeting. Analysts debate the credibility of the negotiations, questioning whether this represents genuine dialogue or a tactical pause before further military action. Concerns are raised about the United States' reputation in negotiations, given past patterns where Iran has been attacked during or after talks. The discussion highlights questions about Iran's internal power dynamics, with uncertainty over whether the Revolutionary Guard or politicians control the situation. Experts suggest that unless substantial outcomes emerge, the perception in the Islamic world could be that Iran has successfully challenged American military might.

- **Newark Liberty International Airport** air traffic controllers evacuated the tower Monday morning after detecting smoke caused by grinding elevator gears, the FAA said.[2][3]
- A ground stop halted all flights until at least 8:30 a.m. as controllers shifted to an on-site backup ramp tower to resume operations.[1][2]
- The disruption compounded a chaotic morning after a fatal **Air Canada** Express crash at **LaGuardia Airport** closed that facility through at least 2 p.m.[4][5]

meta_data.description:
As the partial government shutdown stretches into its fourth week, major airline executives are pressing Congress to resolve the stalemate, warning that the impasse is already disrupting air travel and straining the nation’s airport security system.

Travelers across the country are experiencing significantly longer wait times at airport security checkpoints, with some reporting delays of two to three hours just to pass through Transportation Security Administration screening.

The delays come as TSA employees missed their first full paycheck Friday because of the shutdown — a consequence of the ongoing funding dispute over the Department of Homeland Security.

Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy said the financial strain on frontline security workers is beginning to show.

“These are men and women who don’t make a lot of money,” Duffy said. “Some of them are making choices whether they’re driving Uber or waiting tables. They have to put food on their family’s table.”

Airline leaders say the situation cannot continue. In an open letter sent to lawmakers Sunday, several major airline CEOs urged Congress to act quickly to fund TSA operations and end the shutdown, warning that air travel has once again become “the political football” in Washington.

At the center of the dispute is funding for U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement, the immigration enforcement agency housed within the Department of Homeland Security.

Democrats say they are willing to fund parts of DHS — including TSA, the Coast Guard, and the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency — but want to exclude ICE unless major reforms are made.

Sen. Cory Booker, D-N.J., defended that position, arguing the agency’s actions in communities across the country should not receive additional funding.

“I will not approve another dollar for ICE given all that they're doing,” Booker said in a recent interview. “But we should be funding those TSA agents that keep us safe.”

Republicans have rejected that approach, arguing Congress cannot selectively fund agencies within DHS while leaving others out.

Sen. John Barrasso, the Senate Majority Whip, accused Democrats of undermining national security during a period of heightened global threats.

- **Trump** rejected a proposal from **Thune** on Sunday to fund all DHS agencies except ICE, killing a bipartisan path to ending the partial shutdown, according to Punchbowl News.[2][1]
- TSA workers face a second missed paycheck this week as roughly 300 officers have resigned and airport wait times have stretched to hours at major hubs.[7][4]
- ICE agents began deploying to 14 airports Monday, a move critics including the **ACLU** called reckless given agents' lack of security screening training.[4][1]
